title: Forestry and related workers
description: Conduct research in breeding, physiology, production, yield, and management of crops and agricultural plants or trees, shrubs, and nursery stock, their growth in soils, and control of pests; or study the chemical, physical, biological, and mineralogical composition of soils as they relate to plant or crop growth. May classify and map soils and investigate effects of alternative practices on soil and crop productivity. Manage public and private forested lands for economic, recreational, and conservation purposes. May inventory the type, amount, and location of standing timber, appraise the timber's worth, negotiate the purchase, and draw up contracts for procurement. May determine how to conserve wildlife habitats, creek beds, water quality, and soil stability, and how best to comply with environmental regulations. May devise plans for planting and growing new trees, monitor trees for healthy growth, and determine optimal harvesting schedules. Instruct and advise individuals and families engaged in agriculture, agricultural-related processes, or home management activities. Demonstrate procedures and apply research findings to advance agricultural and home management activities. May develop educational outreach programs. May instruct on either agricultural issues such as agricultural processes and techniques, pest management, and food safety, or on home management issues such as budgeting, nutrition, and child development. (Based on 20 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Monitor contract compliance and results of forestry activities to assure adherence to government regulations.
Collect and evaluate data to determine community program needs and conduct agricultural research, analyzing data and preparing reports.
Collaborate with producers and farmers to diagnose and prevent management and production problems and provide direct assistance in areas like farming techniques.
Conduct classes or deliver lectures on subjects such as nutrition, home management, and farming techniques; develop educational outreach programs.
Maintain records of services provided and the effects of advice given while scheduling regular visits to farmers and providing ongoing support.
Evaluate log characteristics, determine grades, and appraise trees for certain characteristics, using established criteria.
Plan and supervise forestry projects, including determining the type, number, and placement of trees to be planted, while also managing tree nurseries.
Plan and implement projects for conservation of wildlife habitats, soil, and water quality, including mapping forest area soils and vegetation.
Prepare and implement cutting programs and manage timber sales from harvested areas, assisting companies to achieve production goals.
Inspect trees to determine if they have diseases or pest problems, providing recommendations on tree care and conservation practices.
Operate vehicles or powered equipment related to forestry work, including tractors and chainsaws while performing routine maintenance on tools and equipment.
Trim, top, and reshape trees or clear brush from work areas using appropriate tools to enhance forest management and safety.
Negotiate terms of agreements for forest harvesting, management, and leasing of forest lands, ensuring compliance with relevant regulations.
Conduct public educational programs on forest care and conservation and advise private woodlot owners on forestry issues.
Supervise other forestry workers, providing training and oversight to ensure adherence to safety and operational protocols.
Monitor forest-cleared lands to ensure that they are reclaimed for suitable use, maintaining records of changes and conditions.
Gather and remove litter and debris from forestry work areas to maintain safety and cleanliness.
Lock doors and gates of entrances and exits to secure buildings.
Provide directions and respond to passenger inquiries.
Patrol work areas to detect any suspicious items or monitor passenger flow through screening checkpoints to ensure order and efficiency.
Monitor workers to ensure that safety regulations are followed, warning or disciplining those who violate safety regulations.
Drive or operate farm machinery, such as trucks, tractors, or self-propelled harvesters, to transport workers or supplies or to cultivate or harvest fields.
Inspect crops, fields, or plant stock to determine conditions and need for cultivating, spraying, weeding, or harvesting, and record information such as pesticide use or yields.
Train workers in techniques such as planting, harvesting, weeding, or insect identification and in the use of safety measures.
Direct and monitor the work of casual and seasonal help during planting and harvesting, assigning tasks to ensure efficient workflow.
Perform administrative duties, such as authorizing leaves or processing time sheets, and maintain required records such as work activity or personnel reports.
Identify plants, pests, and weeds to determine the selection and application of pesticides and fertilizers, and train workers in pest management.
Inspect completed work to ensure conformance to specifications, standards, and contract requirements, and investigate work-related complaints.
Operate or tend equipment used in agricultural production, including mixing or application of fertilizers, insecticides, herbicides, or fungicides.
Monitor project activities to ensure that instructions are followed, deadlines are met, and schedules are maintained.
Prepare service estimates based on labor, material, and machine costs and maintain budgets for individual projects.
Confer with managers or landscape architects to develop plans or schedules for landscaping maintenance or improvement and evaluate weather or soil conditions to determine plans.
Conduct experiments investigating how soil forms, changes, or interacts with land-based ecosystems or living organisms.
Investigate responses of soils to specific management practices to determine the effects on soil productivity.
Identify degraded or contaminated soils and develop plans to improve their chemical, biological, or physical characteristics.
Provide information or recommendations to farmers or landowners on promoting plant growth or correcting problems like erosion.
Conduct research to determine the best methods of planting, cultivating, harvesting, and storing horticultural products.
Develop improved methods for controlling or eliminating weeds, crop diseases, or insect pests in an environmentally safe way.
Communicate research or project results to professionals or the public; teach related courses or workshops.
Study soil characteristics to classify soils based on factors like geographic location or soil properties.
Develop ways of altering soils to suit different types of plants for better growth and productivity.
Conduct experiments to investigate the underlying mechanisms of plant growth and response to environmental factors.
